program.title: AI Mastery: Interactive Course 2026
program.summary: A 15-module interactive course taking learners from neural-network foundations to production agent swarms — curated video, hands-on Python exercises, authored MCQ quizzes, and capstone projects across every major AI ecosystem.

levels.title: Beginner — Foundation
outcomes: Articulate how LLMs work conceptually and operate multiple AI tools in parallel
Craft effective prompts using structured templates
Develop tool-comparison literacy and basic AI safety awareness

levels.title: Intermediate — Practitioner
outcomes: Build automation scripts and RAG systems with vector databases
Construct graph-based workflows and persistent memory systems
Apply cost optimisation: semantic caching, prompt caching, model routing

levels.title: Advanced — Architect
outcomes: Design autonomous agents and multi-agent orchestration via MCP and A2A
Fine-tune open-weight models and deploy at enterprise scale
Implement defense-in-depth security against the OWASP Agentic Top 10

levels.title: Expert — Strategist
outcomes: Architect cross-ecosystem integration and AI governance at scale
Translate research into production architecture
Evaluate global model ecosystems and hybrid on-device/cloud designs

tracks.title: Claude & Anthropic Ecosystem
tracks.description: Production-grade application development across the Anthropic Claude family — model routing, Claude Code mastery, MCP servers, and Constitutional AI safety.
tracks.targetLearner: Software engineers and technical leads building production applications with Claude; architects designing multi-model routing strategies.

tracks.title: OpenAI Ecosystem
tracks.description: Building agentic applications on OpenAI — the Responses API, Agents SDK, Codex CLI sandboxing, multimodal pipelines, and Assistants API migration.
tracks.focusEcosystem: GPT-5.5, Codex CLI, GPT Image 2, Agents SDK
tracks.targetLearner: Full-stack developers building agentic applications; teams migrating from the Assistants API; engineers deploying voice and multimodal systems.

tracks.title: Google Gemini Ecosystem
tracks.description: Developing on Google's AI platform — Gemini 3.1 inference tiers, AI Studio vibe coding, Vertex AI deployment, A2A agent cards, and media generation.
tracks.focusEcosystem: Gemini 3.1, A2A, Vertex AI, AI Studio
tracks.targetLearner: Developers building on Google Cloud; teams evaluating Gemini for enterprise; architects designing agent orchestration at scale.

tracks.title: Kimi & Chinese AI Ecosystem
tracks.description: The Kimi K2.6 model, 300-agent swarm architecture, open-weight self-hosting, and geopolitical / data-sovereignty considerations for Chinese AI.
tracks.targetLearner: Cost-conscious teams evaluating open-weight and Chinese models; architects designing hybrid US/China model stacks.

tracks.title: Hermes & Local AI
tracks.description: Self-hosted autonomous agents with Hermes — closed learning loops, multi-platform gateways, defense-in-depth security, and local model inference.
tracks.targetLearner: Teams requiring self-hosted agents for compliance; cost-sensitive deployments; non-dev PM/Ops teams adopting automation.

tracks.title: Image & Video Generation
tracks.description: Production image and video generation — six-part prompting, model selection, ComfyUI workflows, local FLUX, and commercial licensing / IP risk.
tracks.targetLearner: Designers and engineers building media-generation pipelines; teams evaluating commercial licensing risk.

tracks.title: Agentic AI & Orchestration
tracks.description: Autonomous agent design and multi-agent orchestration — MCP/A2A protocols, LangGraph state machines, swarm topologies, and OWASP ASI defences.
tracks.targetLearner: Senior developers and architects designing autonomous and multi-agent systems for production.

tracks.title: Prompt Engineering & System Design
tracks.description: Systematic prompt engineering — zero/few-shot, CoT, ReAct, meta-prompting, prompt registries, regression testing, and injection-defence strategy.
tracks.targetLearner: Engineers and prompt specialists building reliable, evaluated, version-controlled prompt systems.

tracks.title: Native App AI Integration
tracks.description: On-device AI in native and cross-platform apps — Apple Intelligence, Gemini Nano/AICore, ExecuTorch, and hybrid on-device/cloud architectures.
tracks.focusEcosystem: iOS (Apple Intelligence), Android (Gemini Nano), React Native
tracks.targetLearner: Mobile engineers integrating on-device and hybrid AI into iOS, Android, and React Native apps.

tracks.title: AI-Powered Web & UX
tracks.description: AI-assisted web and UX — WCAG 3.0 accessibility, Core Web Vitals, AI-generated UI with compliance, and structured data at scale.
tracks.focusEcosystem: WCAG 3.0, Core Web Vitals, AI-assisted design
tracks.targetLearner: Front-end engineers and designers building accessible, performant, AI-assisted web experiences.

tracks.title: Senior Engineering Practices
tracks.description: Senior engineering for AI systems — Clean Architecture, SOLID, DevSecOps pipelines, multi-environment model promotion, and rollback strategy.
tracks.focusEcosystem: Clean Architecture, DevSecOps, CI/CD for AI
tracks.targetLearner: Senior engineers and tech leads establishing engineering standards for AI systems.

tracks.title: Neural Network Fundamentals
tracks.description: Neural network fundamentals for practitioners — transformers and attention, diffusion, LoRA fine-tuning, quantisation, and hybrid inference design.
tracks.targetLearner: Engineers who need working knowledge of model internals to fine-tune and deploy effectively.

lessons.title: Prompt Engineering Mastery
lessons.summary: Master fundamental and advanced prompting techniques

Structure prompts using XML tags (Anthropic best practice)
Implement chain-of-thought and ReAct patterns
Build reusable prompt libraries for consistent results

Token economy: Every word costs money
Assigning a specific role improves output quality
Persona design: Creating consistent character profiles

Tone and style control through persona

exercises.title: Technical Documentation
exercises.instructions: Complete the hands-on exercise: Technical Documentation.
exercises.starterCode:
Task: Document a REST API endpoint

Steps:
1. Paste your code for the endpoint
2. Ask Claude: "Generate API documentation for this endpoint
   following OpenAPI 3.0 spec. Include parameters, request/response
   examples, and error codes."
3. Review and refine: "Add authentication details" or "Include
   rate limiting information"
4. Export as an Artifact in markdown format
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

exercises.title: Complete Business Workflow
exercises.instructions: Create a Claude Project called "Weekly Report Automation" and set it up with: 1. **Upload these files** (create sample data): - `sales-data.csv` - Weekly sales figures - `meeting-notes.md` - Notes from team meetings - `goals.md` - Monthly goals and KPIs 2. **Set Project Instructions:**
exercises.starterCode:
You are my weekly report assistant. Each week, I will upload
new data and ask you to generate a comprehensive report.

The report should include:
1. Executive summary (3-4 sentences)
2. Key metrics vs. goals (table format)
3. Notable wins and concerns
4. Action items for next week
5. Questions that need team discussion

Use a professional but approachable tone.
Format everything in clean markdown with tables where appropriate.
exercises.expectedOutcome: 3. **Run the workflow** 3 times with different inputs to refine 4. **Save the best output** as a template for future use

exercises.title: Your First Neural Network
exercises.instructions: Complete the hands-on exercise: Your First Neural Network.
exercises.starterCode:
# Install required packages:
# pip install numpy tensorflow

import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

# Step 1: Create a simple dataset
# Problem: Predict whether a number is positive or negative
X = np.array([[-5], [-3], [-1], [0], [1], [3], [5], [10], [-10], [2]])
y = np.array([[0], [0], [0], [0], [1], [1], [1], [1], [0], [1]])  # 0=negative, 1=positive

# Step 2: Build the neural network
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.Dense(4, activation='relu', input_shape=(1,)),  # Hidden layer with 4 neurons
    layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')  # Output layer (binary classification)
])

# Step 3: Compile the model
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# Step 4: Train
print("Training the neural network...")
model.fit(X, y, epochs=200, verbose=0)

# Step 5: Test predictions
test_numbers = np.array([[-7], [4], [0.5], [-2]])
predictions = model.predict(test_numbers, verbose=0)

print("\nPredictions (closer to 1 = positive, closer to 0 = negative):")
for num, pred in zip(test_numbers, predictions):
    label = "positive" if pred[0] > 0.5 else "negative"
    confidence = pred[0] if pred[0] > 0.5 else 1 - pred[0]
    print(f"  {num[0]:>4} → {pred[0]:.4f} → {label} ({confidence:.1%} confidence)")

print("\n--- KEY INSIGHT ---")
print("This tiny network learned to classify numbers without being explicitly programmed!")
print("Real LLMs use the same fundamental principles, just with billions of parameters.")
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

exercises.title: Understanding Tokens
exercises.instructions: Complete the hands-on exercise: Understanding Tokens.
exercises.starterCode:
# pip install tiktoken

import tiktoken

# See how text is broken into tokens (the unit LLMs work with)
encoder = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4")

texts = [
    "Hello, world!",
    "Artificial Intelligence",
    "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog",
    "AI",  # Short words may be 1 token
    "antidisestablishmentarianism",  # Long words = many tokens
]

for text in texts:
    tokens = encoder.encode(text)
    print(f"'{text}' → {len(tokens)} tokens: {tokens}")
    # Decode each token individually
    decoded = [encoder.decode([t]) for t in tokens]
    print(f"  Token breakdown: {decoded}\n")
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

keyConcepts: Claude Code is a terminal-based AI coding assistant
Authentication: OAuth with your Anthropic account
Workspace requirements: Must be in a Git repository
Context window: The amount of text Claude can "see" at once
Token budget: Every file read, every message consumes tokens ($)
Efficient context = lower costs + better results
`/clear`: Reset context when it gets bloated
Commands can run shell scripts, open files, or trigger workflows
Combine with Git hooks for automation
MCP (Model Context Protocol) extends Claude Code's capabilities

exercises.title: Build a Task Manager API
exercises.instructions: **Goal:** Build a RESTful task manager API using Claude Code **Tech Stack:** Node.js + Express + SQLite
exercises.starterCode:
# Step 1: Create project directory and initialize
mkdir task-manager && cd task-manager
git init

# Step 2: Create CLAUDE.md
# [Paste the CLAUDE.md template from section 5.8]

# Step 3: Start Claude Code in Plan Mode
claude /plan

# Step 4: Give the task
"Build a RESTful task manager API with these endpoints:
- GET /tasks (list all, with optional ?status= filter)
- GET /tasks/:id (get one)
- POST /tasks (create)
- PUT /tasks/:id (update)
- DELETE /tasks/:id (remove)
- POST /tasks/:id/complete (mark complete)

Use Express.js with an in-memory store (we'll add SQLite later).
Include input validation, error handling, and basic tests."

# Step 5: Review the plan, approve it

# Step 6: Watch Claude implement, review each change

# Step 7: Run the tests
npm test

# Step 8: If tests fail, ask Claude to fix

# Step 9: Add a skill for future CRUD APIs
# Save as .claude/skills/crud-api.md
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

exercises.title: Your First Neural Network
exercises.instructions: Complete the hands-on exercise: Your First Neural Network.
exercises.starterCode:
# Install required packages:
# pip install numpy tensorflow

import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow import keras
from tensorflow.keras import layers

# Step 1: Create a simple dataset
# Problem: Predict whether a number is positive or negative
X = np.array([[-5], [-3], [-1], [0], [1], [3], [5], [10], [-10], [2]])
y = np.array([[0], [0], [0], [0], [1], [1], [1], [1], [0], [1]])  # 0=negative, 1=positive

# Step 2: Build the neural network
model = keras.Sequential([
    layers.Dense(4, activation='relu', input_shape=(1,)),  # Hidden layer with 4 neurons
    layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid')  # Output layer (binary classification)
])

# Step 3: Compile the model
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='binary_crossentropy', metrics=['accuracy'])

# Step 4: Train
print("Training the neural network...")
model.fit(X, y, epochs=200, verbose=0)

# Step 5: Test predictions
test_numbers = np.array([[-7], [4], [0.5], [-2]])
predictions = model.predict(test_numbers, verbose=0)

print("\nPredictions (closer to 1 = positive, closer to 0 = negative):")
for num, pred in zip(test_numbers, predictions):
    label = "positive" if pred[0] > 0.5 else "negative"
    confidence = pred[0] if pred[0] > 0.5 else 1 - pred[0]
    print(f"  {num[0]:>4} → {pred[0]:.4f} → {label} ({confidence:.1%} confidence)")

print("\n--- KEY INSIGHT ---")
print("This tiny network learned to classify numbers without being explicitly programmed!")
print("Real LLMs use the same fundamental principles, just with billions of parameters.")
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

exercises.title: Understanding Tokens
exercises.instructions: Complete the hands-on exercise: Understanding Tokens.
exercises.starterCode:
# pip install tiktoken

import tiktoken

# See how text is broken into tokens (the unit LLMs work with)
encoder = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("gpt-4")

texts = [
    "Hello, world!",
    "Artificial Intelligence",
    "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog",
    "AI",  # Short words may be 1 token
    "antidisestablishmentarianism",  # Long words = many tokens
]

for text in texts:
    tokens = encoder.encode(text)
    print(f"'{text}' → {len(tokens)} tokens: {tokens}")
    # Decode each token individually
    decoded = [encoder.decode([t]) for t in tokens]
    print(f"  Token breakdown: {decoded}\n")
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

exercises.title: Prompt Engineering for Images
exercises.instructions: Write 5 prompts for the same subject with different styles:
exercises.starterCode:
Subject: A futuristic city

1. Photorealistic: "A futuristic city skyline at dusk, photorealistic,
   8K, shot on Sony A7IV, 24mm lens, dramatic clouds, neon lights
   reflecting on wet streets"

2. Anime: "A futuristic city in anime style, Studio Ghibli inspired,
   hand-drawn aesthetic, vibrant colors, flying vehicles,
   cherry blossom trees, warm lighting"

3. Watercolor: "A futuristic city, watercolor painting style,
   soft edges, pastel colors, impressionistic, dreamy atmosphere"

4. Cyberpunk: "A cyberpunk city, neon-soaked streets, rain,
   holographic advertisements, dystopian atmosphere,
   Blade Runner style, cinematic"

5. Minimalist: "A futuristic city, minimalist design,
   clean lines, white and gold color palette,
   geometric shapes, architectural visualization"
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.

exercises.title: Complete Business Workflow
exercises.instructions: Create a Claude Project called "Weekly Report Automation" and set it up with: 1. **Upload these files** (create sample data): - `sales-data.csv` - Weekly sales figures - `meeting-notes.md` - Notes from team meetings - `goals.md` - Monthly goals and KPIs 2. **Set Project Instructions:**
exercises.starterCode:
You are my weekly report assistant. Each week, I will upload
new data and ask you to generate a comprehensive report.

The report should include:
1. Executive summary (3-4 sentences)
2. Key metrics vs. goals (table format)
3. Notable wins and concerns
4. Action items for next week
5. Questions that need team discussion

Use a professional but approachable tone.
Format everything in clean markdown with tables where appropriate.
exercises.expectedOutcome: 3. **Run the workflow** 3 times with different inputs to refine 4. **Save the best output** as a template for future use

exercises.title: Build a Task Manager API
exercises.instructions: **Goal:** Build a RESTful task manager API using Claude Code **Tech Stack:** Node.js + Express + SQLite
exercises.starterCode:
# Step 1: Create project directory and initialize
mkdir task-manager && cd task-manager
git init

# Step 2: Create CLAUDE.md
# [Paste the CLAUDE.md template from section 5.8]

# Step 3: Start Claude Code in Plan Mode
claude /plan

# Step 4: Give the task
"Build a RESTful task manager API with these endpoints:
- GET /tasks (list all, with optional ?status= filter)
- GET /tasks/:id (get one)
- POST /tasks (create)
- PUT /tasks/:id (update)
- DELETE /tasks/:id (remove)
- POST /tasks/:id/complete (mark complete)

Use Express.js with an in-memory store (we'll add SQLite later).
Include input validation, error handling, and basic tests."

# Step 5: Review the plan, approve it

# Step 6: Watch Claude implement, review each change

# Step 7: Run the tests
npm test

# Step 8: If tests fail, ask Claude to fix

# Step 9: Add a skill for future CRUD APIs
# Save as .claude/skills/crud-api.md
exercises.expectedOutcome: Run the code and confirm it produces the described result; reason about why each step works.
